summary: Stellar New Potato & Dark Star; tight early‑68 set
blurb: The four‑song core of the show roars with a scorching New Potato Caboose and an expansive Dark Star, while Death Don't Have No Mercy and Good Morning Little School Girl deliver raw blues power. Jerry’s guitar and vocals are on fire, and Pigpen’s organ cuts through with crystal clarity.
review: On March 29, 1968 at San Francisco’s Carousel Ballroom the Dead were in full exploratory mode. The set opens with a gritty Good Morning Little School Girl that immediately sets a blues‑heavy tone, then dives into the gut‑wrenching Death Don't Have No Mercy before launching into a raucous Sitting On Top Of The World. Dark Star follows, soaring on crystal‑clear matrix sound that captures every swirling organ note and Jerry’s sublime lead work; it flows into a haunting Morning Dew and a brief but fiery Turn On Your Lovelight segue. The climax arrives with New Potato Caboose – a howling, sustained guitar showcase that many reviewers call “top‑of‑the‑line,” before the band closes with the quirky Born Cross‑Eyed. Across three excellent recordings (matrix and two audience tapes) the performance is consistently praised: Jerry’s solos are described as sublime, Phil’s bass deep and impressive (if a bit low in the mix), and Pigpen’s organ cursing shines through. The drummers lock into a solid, underwater‑feel groove that propels the improvisations without ever getting sloppy. All three sources agree this is one of the strongest early‑68 shows, making it a must‑listen for fans of the band’s formative psychedelic period.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All three recordings are uniformly positive, repeatedly praising New Potato Caboose, Dark Star, Death Don't Have No Mercy and Jerry's guitar/vocals. Consistent high‑energy descriptions across matrix and audience tapes indicate strong consensus of exceptional playing.

best_recording.reason: Early GD‑created matrix capture with crystal‑clear sound; superb detail on Dark Star and New Potato Caboose, minimal crowd bleed.

key_highlights: Superb early matrix fidelity that places the listener in the ballroom.
Incendiary New Potato Caboose and soaring Dark Star are show‑stoppers.
Raw blues power on Death Don't Have No Mercy and Good Morning Little School Girl.
song_highlights: Sitting On Top Of The World

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers sublime, top‑of‑the‑line guitar solos on New Potato and Dark Star, with deep‑throated vocals that shine especially on Death Don't Have No Mercy.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass is deep and impressive, though a few recordings note it sits low in the mix; he also adds a spoken quip after Sitting On Top Of The World.
band_performance.Brent: Pigpen’s organ cuts through with crystal‑clear tone on Dark Star and even cursing in the Lovelight excerpt, adding raw 1968 texture.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart’s drumming provides a solid, slightly underwater groove that propels the improvisations without any noticeable flaws.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann locks in tightly with Hart, delivering steady rhythmic support throughout the high‑energy set.

must_listen_sequences: Sitting On Top Of The World
